meta.purpose: Reel-sized audio scripts (~30-40s) for social media posting.
meta.grouping_rules: Chapter boundaries always break a reel. Verses greedily fill up to ~95 words (ideal ~80). Consecutive verses by the same speaker collapse into a single segment; speaker changes start a new segment within the same reel.
meta.tts_usage: For each reel, generate each segment separately (one Gemini call per segment with its own voice), then concatenate the PCM output client-side. Alternatively, for reels with <=2 speakers, use Gemini's multi-speaker TTS mode in a single call.

segments.instructions: Read this plainly and calmly, at a normal pace. A slightly older voice, but not theatrical — just a king asking a question.
segments.text: Sanjaya, tell me — my sons and the sons of Pandu gathered on the sacred field of Kurukshetra, ready to fight. What did they do?
segments.prompt:

segments.speaker: Sanjaya
segments.instructions: Read this in an even, straightforward narrator's voice, at a normal conversational pace. Plainly stated, no dramatic emphasis.
segments.text:
Seeing the Pandava army drawn up for battle, King Duryodhana went to his teacher Drona and said:

"Look, teacher, at this great Pandava army, arranged by your own brilliant student, Drupada's son."

"Here are heroes, mighty archers — a match in battle for Bhima and Arjuna — Yuyudhana, Virata, and Drupada, the great charioteer;"

"Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, and the brave king of Kashi; Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and Shaibya, a leader of men;"

"the valiant Yudhamanyu, the powerful Uttamaujas, Subhadra's son, and Draupadi's sons — all great warriors."

"Now let me name, O best of the twice-born, the best warriors on our side, the leaders of my army — listen:"

"You yourself, Bhishma, Karna, Kripa — all victorious in battle; Ashvatthama, Vikarna, and Somadatta's son;"

"and many other heroes, skilled with many weapons, all battle-tested and ready to die for me."

"Our army, led by Bhishma, is immense; theirs, led by Bhima, is limited."

"So all of you, holding your positions at every point in the formation, protect Bhishma above everyone else."

segments.text:
Then, to cheer Duryodhana, the old grandfather of the Kurus, Bhishma, roared like a lion and blew his conch loudly.

Then conches, kettledrums, tabors, trumpets, and horns blared out all at once — a tremendous noise.

Then, standing in their great chariot yoked to white horses, Krishna and Arjuna blew their divine conches.

Krishna blew his conch Panchajanya; Arjuna blew Devadatta; and Bhima of terrifying deeds blew the great conch Paundra.

King Yudhishthira blew Anantavijaya; Nakula and Sahadeva blew Sughosha and Manipushpaka.

The king of Kashi, a supreme archer; Shikhandi, the great warrior; Dhrishtadyumna, Virata, and the unconquered Satyaki;

Drupada, the sons of Draupadi, and Subhadra's mighty son — O king, each of them blew his own conch.

That tremendous sound, echoing through sky and earth, shattered the hearts of Dhritarashtra's sons.

segments.speaker: Arjuna
segments.instructions: Read this slightly softer than normal, at natural pace. Plain honest delivery, no performance.
segments.text:
Krishna, seeing my own family here, ready to fight, my limbs give way and my mouth runs dry.

My body shakes, my hair stands on end, my bow Gandiva slips from my hand, and my skin is burning.

I can't stand steady; my mind is reeling. I see bad omens, Krishna.

I see nothing good in killing my own people in battle. I don't want victory, or a kingdom, or pleasures, Krishna.

What use is a kingdom to us, Govinda? What use are enjoyments or even life, when the very people for whose sake we want them

are standing here ready to fight, giving up their lives and wealth — teachers, fathers, sons, grandfathers,

uncles, fathers-in-law, grandsons, brothers-in-law, other relatives? Krishna, I don't want to kill them, even if they kill me — not for all three worlds, much less for this earth.

What joy would we get, Krishna, from killing Dhritarashtra's sons? Only sin would stain us, even though they are the aggressors.

segments.text:
So it's wrong for us to kill Dhritarashtra's sons — our own relatives. How could we be happy, Krishna, after killing our own family?

Even if they, with minds clouded by greed, see nothing wrong in destroying a family or betraying friends —

why shouldn't we, who clearly see the evil of destroying a family, turn away from this sin?

When a family is destroyed, its ancient traditions are lost. When tradition is lost, lawlessness takes over the whole family.

When lawlessness dominates, the women become corrupted; and when women are corrupted, social order breaks down.

This confusion leads the destroyers of the family — and the family itself — to ruin, for the ancestors, deprived of the offerings due to them, also fall.

Through the sins of these family-destroyers, which cause the collapse of social order, the ancient customs of caste and family are uprooted.

Where has this weakness come from, Arjuna, at such a critical moment? It doesn't suit a noble man. It won't bring heaven, and it won't bring honor.

Don't give in to this cowardice — it isn't you. Shake off this petty weakness of heart and stand up, Arjuna.

segments.text:
Krishna, how can I shoot arrows at Bhishma and Drona on the battlefield? They deserve my respect, not my weapons.

Better to live by begging in this world than to kill these great teachers. If I kill them — even though they seek their own gain — everything I enjoy will be stained with their blood.

And I don't know which is better — that we defeat them or they defeat us. Those very sons of Dhritarashtra stand there, and if I kill them I won't want to live.

My mind is clouded by weak pity; I'm confused about what's right. Tell me clearly what's best for me. I am your student. Teach me — I take refuge in you.

segments.text:
You grieve for those who should not be grieved for — and yet you speak words of wisdom. The wise grieve for neither the living nor the dead.

There was never a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor these kings. Nor will any of us cease to exist in the future.

Just as the soul within this body passes through childhood, youth, and old age, it passes into another body after death. The wise are not troubled by this.

Contact with the world brings cold and heat, pleasure and pain — they come and go, they don't last. Endure them, Arjuna.

The person these things don't disturb — the one steady in pleasure and pain — that wise person is ready for immortality.

segments.text:
What isn't real can never truly exist; what is real can never stop existing. Those who see the truth see both clearly.

Know that what pervades all this is indestructible. No one can destroy what does not pass away.

These bodies have an end — but the embodied one within them is eternal, indestructible, beyond measure. So fight, Arjuna.

Anyone who thinks the soul kills, or that the soul is killed — neither understands. The soul does not kill and is not killed.

The soul is never born and never dies. Once it exists, it does not cease to be. Unborn, eternal, everlasting, ancient — it is not killed when the body is killed.

segments.text:
Arjuna, if someone knows the soul is indestructible, eternal, unborn, unchanging — how can such a person kill anyone or cause anyone to kill?

Just as a person throws away worn-out clothes and puts on new ones, the soul throws away worn-out bodies and takes on new ones.

Weapons cannot cut the soul, fire cannot burn it, water cannot wet it, wind cannot dry it.

It cannot be cut, burned, wetted, or dried. It is eternal, everywhere, stable, unmoving, forever.

The soul is beyond the reach of the senses, beyond thought, unchanging. Knowing this, you shouldn't grieve.

And even if you think the soul is constantly being born and dying, you still have no reason to grieve, Arjuna.

Whoever is born must die; whoever dies must be born again. There's no point grieving over the unavoidable.

Beings are unseen before they appear, visible in the middle, and unseen again when they go. Why grieve about this?

Some look at the soul as a wonder; some speak of it as a wonder; others hear about it as a wonder — and even after hearing, no one truly knows it.

The one within every body is eternal and indestructible, Arjuna. So there's no reason to grieve for any living being.

Look at it from the standpoint of your own duty, and you won't hesitate. For a warrior there is nothing better than a righteous battle.

Fortunate are the warriors, Arjuna, who are offered a battle like this that comes of its own accord — an open door to heaven.

segments.text:
But if you refuse to fight this righteous battle, you'll abandon your duty and your honor — and you'll earn only sin.

People will speak of your lasting disgrace, and for an honored man, dishonor is worse than death.

The great warriors will think you withdrew out of fear, and those who once respected you will hold you in contempt.

Your enemies will say cruel things, mocking your strength. What could be more painful than that?

If you're killed, you'll reach heaven; if you win, you'll rule the earth. So stand up, Arjuna, with the resolve to fight.

Treat pleasure and pain, gain and loss, victory and defeat as equal, and then fight. This way you won't incur any sin.

This wisdom I've described is from the standpoint of knowledge; now hear it from the standpoint of action (yoga). Armed with this, you'll break free from the bondage of karma.

On this path, no effort is wasted, no progress is undone. Even a little of this practice protects you from great fear.

On this path, Arjuna, the mind is focused — one resolution, one direction. But the minds of the unresolved scatter endlessly in many directions.

segments.text:
The unwise get carried away with the flowery passages of the Vedas, declaring "there's nothing more than this,"

full of desire, aiming at heaven, offering rituals for rebirth and results — loaded with specific ceremonies chasing pleasure and power.

For people attached to pleasure and power, their minds swept away by such talk, the focused, meditative mind never settles.

The Vedas deal with the three qualities of nature (sattva, rajas, tamas). Rise above them, Arjuna — beyond the pairs of opposites, steady in purity, free from concerns of gain and loss, self-possessed.

A well is of little use when water is flooding everywhere. Similarly, all the Vedas are of little use to the one who truly knows.

You have a right to your action, but never to its results. Don't let the results be your motivation, and don't cling to inaction either.

Do your work steadily, Arjuna, letting go of attachment, indifferent to success and failure. This evenness of mind is what yoga means.

Action driven by results is far inferior to action done with wisdom. Take refuge in wisdom. Pitiable are those who act just for results.

segments.text:
When a person lets go of all the desires of the mind and finds contentment in the Self alone, they are called a person of steady wisdom.

Not shaken by sorrow, not craving pleasures, free from desire, fear, and anger — such a person is called a sage of steady mind.

Unattached to anything, neither pleased by good nor upset by bad — that person's wisdom is firmly established.

When a person can pull back their senses from objects the way a tortoise draws in its limbs — their wisdom is firmly established.

Objects fall away from the person who abstains, but the craving for them lingers — until they experience what is higher. Then even the craving goes.

The restless senses, Arjuna, can forcibly carry off the mind even of a wise person who is trying.

segments.text:
So a person should hold all the senses in check and sit focused, devoted to Me. When the senses are under control, wisdom is firmly established.

Dwelling on sense objects breeds attachment to them; attachment breeds desire; desire breeds anger.

Anger breeds delusion; delusion breeds loss of memory; loss of memory breeds destruction of reason; and with reason destroyed, the person is lost.

But a disciplined person moving among sense objects, senses under control, free from attachment and aversion, guided by the Self — attains inner peace.

In peace, all sorrows are destroyed. For the peaceful-minded, steady wisdom quickly takes root.

The undisciplined have no understanding and no meditation. Without meditation, no peace; without peace, how can there be happiness?

When the mind follows wandering senses, understanding is carried off — like a boat being swept away by the wind on water.

segments.text:
So, Arjuna, the person whose senses are completely withdrawn from their objects is the one whose wisdom is firmly established.

What is night for all others, the self-controlled is awake in; what others are awake in, that is night for the sage who sees.

The ocean stays full and undisturbed though rivers pour into it. Likewise, the person desires flow into but who is not disturbed by them reaches peace — not the one who chases desires.

The person who gives up all desires, walks without longing, lets go of "mine" and "I" — that person reaches peace.

This is the state of union with the infinite (Brahman), Arjuna. Reaching it, one is no longer confused. Staying in it even at death, one attains the ultimate peace.

You don't become free from action by simply avoiding action, and you don't reach perfection by renouncing alone.

segments.text:
But the person who controls the senses with the mind and engages the body in action without attachment — that person is excellent, Arjuna.

Do your assigned work. Action is better than inaction. You couldn't even keep your body alive without action.

This world is bound by action — except action done as a sacred offering. So do your work as an offering, Arjuna, without attachment.

In the beginning, the Lord of beings created humans along with sacrifice and said: "By this you shall multiply. Let this fulfill your needs."

"Through this, nourish the gods, and the gods will nourish you. Supporting each other, you'll attain the highest good."

"The gods, nourished by your offerings, will give you what you need. Whoever enjoys their gifts without giving back is a thief."

Good people who eat what's left from an offering are freed from all sins. But those who cook only for themselves eat only sin.

Beings come from food; food comes from rain; rain comes from sacrifice; and sacrifice comes from action.

Action arises from the Vedic principle, which arises from the Imperishable. So the all-pervading infinite is always present in sacrifice.

But the person who finds joy in the Self, is satisfied in the Self, content in the Self alone — has nothing more to do.

Such a person gains nothing from doing and loses nothing from not doing. They depend on no being for anything.

King Janaka and others reached perfection purely through action. You too should act — if only to keep the world going.

Whatever a great person does, others follow. Whatever standard they set, the world imitates.

Arjuna, there's nothing in the three worlds I have to do, nothing I haven't already attained — yet I keep acting.

If I didn't tirelessly keep acting, people everywhere would follow my lead.

If I didn't act, these worlds would fall apart. I would be the cause of chaos, destroying all creatures.

The ignorant act attached to their work, Arjuna. The wise should act just as actively — but without attachment — for the welfare of the world.

A wise person shouldn't unsettle the ignorant who are attached to their work. Instead, acting with full engagement himself, he should inspire them to act well.

Actions are carried out by nature's qualities in every way. But the person deluded by ego thinks, "I am the doer."

Knowing this, the ancient seekers of liberation also performed action. So do your work, just as the ancients did long ago.

What is action? What is inaction? Even the wise are confused about this. I'll tell you clearly, so that you may be freed from harm.

You must understand the nature of right action, of wrong action, and of inaction. The way of action is deep and hard to see.

The person who sees inaction in action and action in inaction — that person is wise, truly yoked, truly active.

The person whose every undertaking is free from desire-based plans, whose actions are burned up in the fire of wisdom — the wise call that person truly learned.

Giving up attachment to results, always content, relying on nothing — even when doing things, such a person doesn't really do anything.

Without expectation, with mind and self controlled, giving up all possessions, acting with only the body — such a person incurs no sin.

Content with whatever comes unsought, beyond pairs of opposites, free from jealousy, even in success and failure — though they act, they are not bound.

For one who is free, whose mind is established in wisdom, who acts as an offering, all karma dissolves.

segments.text:
The offering is Brahman; the sacrifice is Brahman; poured by Brahman into the fire of Brahman. Brahman alone is reached by the one who sees Brahman in all action.

Some yogis offer sacrifices to the gods; others offer the sacrifice itself as an offering into the fire of Brahman.

Some offer the senses — hearing and the rest — into fires of self-discipline. Others offer sense objects — sound and the rest — into the fires of the senses.

Some offer all sense-activities and life-energies into the fire of self-mastery, kindled by wisdom.

Some sacrifice with wealth, some with austerity, some with yoga, some with study and wisdom — people of strict vows.

Others, practicing breath control, regulate the outflow and inflow, offering the outbreath into the inbreath and vice versa.

Others, restricting their food, offer their life-energies into their life-energies. All of these know sacrifice, and their sins are destroyed through sacrifice.

Those who eat the leftovers of sacrifice — the true nectar — reach eternal Brahman. This world isn't even for the non-sacrificer — how could any other world be?

So many kinds of sacrifices are spread out at the mouth of Brahman. Know them all as born of action; knowing this, you'll be free.

The sacrifice of wisdom is better than the sacrifice of material things, Arjuna. Every action, in its fullness, culminates in wisdom.

Seek that wisdom with humility, by asking questions, and by serving. Those who have seen the truth will teach you.

Having grasped this, Arjuna, you'll never fall into confusion again. You'll see every being in yourself — and then in me.

Even if you were the greatest of all sinners, you would cross over all evil on the boat of wisdom.

As a blazing fire reduces firewood to ashes, Arjuna, the fire of wisdom reduces all actions to ashes.

Nothing here purifies like wisdom. A person perfected in yoga finds it within themselves, in time.

A person with faith, committed to practice, senses under control — attains wisdom. And once attained, it soon brings supreme peace.

But the ignorant, the faithless, the doubter — they are lost. For the doubter there is no happiness here, elsewhere, or anywhere.

Arjuna, actions do not bind the person who has given up action through yoga, whose doubts have been cut away by wisdom, who is self-possessed.

So, with the sword of wisdom, cut away this doubt in your heart — born of ignorance. Take up yoga and stand up, Arjuna.

segments.text:
Both renunciation and the way of action lead to the highest. But of the two, the way of action is better than renouncing action.

Know this person as a lifelong renunciate: one who neither hates nor desires. Free from opposites, Arjuna, such a person is easily set free from bondage.

Only children — not the wise — think of the paths of knowledge and action as different. Whoever is fully established in one gets the fruit of both.

The state reached by the path of knowledge is the same state reached by the path of action. The one who sees these two as one — truly sees.

segments.text:
But renunciation without the practice of yoga is hard to attain, Arjuna. The sage committed to yoga reaches the infinite quickly.

Engaged in yoga, purified in self, with body and senses mastered, one's self becoming the Self of all beings — such a person isn't stained, even while acting.

The person who knows the truth thinks: "I'm not really doing anything," even while seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, walking, sleeping, breathing,

speaking, releasing, grasping, opening and closing the eyes — understanding that it's the senses moving among their objects.

The person who acts without attachment, offering actions to Brahman, is not stained by sin — like a lotus leaf untouched by water.

With body, mind, intellect, or even just the senses alone, yogis act without attachment, to purify themselves.

The yogi, letting go of results, reaches lasting peace. The non-yogi, attached to results by desire, is trapped.

Mentally letting go of all actions, the self-controlled person rests peacefully in the "city of nine gates" (the body) — neither acting nor causing action.

The indwelling Self doesn't create agency, actions, or the link of action to result. It's nature (prakriti) that acts.

segments.text:
The all-pervading One doesn't take on anyone's sin or merit. Wisdom is hidden by ignorance, and so beings are confused.

But for those whose ignorance is destroyed by self-knowledge, that knowledge lights up the supreme — like the sun.

With their minds on That, their selves rooted in That, with That as their goal, devoted to That — they reach a state from which they don't return, their sins wiped clean by wisdom.

The wise see the same reality in a learned and humble brahmin, a cow, an elephant, a dog, or even an outcaste.

Those whose minds are steady in seeing sameness have conquered rebirth even here. Brahman is spotless and the same everywhere — so they are established in Brahman.

The one who knows Brahman — steady in intellect, unconfused — doesn't get excited over what is pleasant or upset by what is unpleasant.

With the self unattached to external things, such a person finds the joy that lies in the Self. Joined in yoga with Brahman, they enjoy imperishable happiness.

Pleasures born of sense-contact are wombs of pain. They have a beginning and an end. The wise do not take their delight in them, Arjuna.

segments.text:
Whoever, even here and now, before leaving the body, can withstand the surges of desire and anger — that person is truly yoked; that person is happy.

The one whose happiness, delight, and light are all within — that yogi, becoming Brahman, reaches the peace of Brahman.

Sages whose sins are gone, whose doubts are dispelled, whose minds are disciplined, who delight in the welfare of all beings — they reach the peace of Brahman.

For the disciplined ones free from desire and anger, with minds under control, knowing the Self — the peace of Brahman is close at hand, all around.

Shutting out external stimuli, focusing the gaze between the eyebrows, balancing the inbreath and the outbreath moving through the nostrils,

with senses, mind, and intellect under control, the sage aims at liberation, free from desire, fear, and anger — truly, always free.

segments.text:
The person who does the work they ought to do without depending on the results — that person is the true renunciate and the true yogi; not just the one who lights no ritual fire or does no action.

Know that what's called renunciation is also yoga, Arjuna. No one becomes a yogi without giving up self-serving intentions.

For a sage trying to climb to yoga, action is the means. For one who has reached yoga, stillness is the means.

When a person isn't attached to sense objects or to actions, having given up all self-serving intentions — then they're said to have reached yoga.

Lift yourself by yourself. Don't let yourself fall. The Self alone is your friend; the self alone is your enemy.

For the person who has conquered themselves, the Self is a friend. But to the one who hasn't, their own self acts like an enemy.

A self-controlled, peaceful person remains steady in the Supreme Self — through cold and heat, pleasure and pain, honor and dishonor.

The yogi who is content with knowledge and realization, unshakable, senses under control — to whom a clod of earth, a stone, and a lump of gold are alike — is truly yoked.

segments.text:
Whoever sees with equal mind well-wishers, friends, enemies, neutrals, mediators, the hateful, relatives, the righteous, and even the sinful — excels.

Let the yogi constantly concentrate the mind, sitting alone in a quiet place, with body and mind under control, without possessions, without cravings.

Setting up a steady seat for oneself in a clean place, neither too high nor too low, covered with cloth, deerskin, and kusha grass, layered together,

sitting there, making the mind one-pointed, controlling the activities of mind and senses, let the yogi practice to purify the self.

Holding the body, head, and neck erect and still, gazing steadily at the tip of the nose, not looking around,

with a serene mind, fearless, firm in a vow of celibacy, mind controlled, thoughts fixed on me — let the yogi sit, absorbed, with me as the goal.

Always yoking the mind this way, the yogi of disciplined mind reaches the peace abiding in me — the peace that ends in liberation.

Yoga is not for someone who eats too much or eats nothing at all, not for the one who sleeps too much or stays awake too much, Arjuna.

let the person gradually grow still with an intellect held steady. With the mind fixed on the Self, let them think of nothing at all.

Arjuna, with your mind fixed on me, taking refuge in me, practicing yoga — listen to how you can know me completely, beyond doubt.

I'll tell you fully the knowledge with realization — knowing which there's nothing else here left to know.

Among thousands of people, maybe one strives for perfection. Even among those who strive and attain it, maybe one truly knows me.

Earth, water, fire, air, space, mind, intellect, and ego — these are the eight divisions of my lower nature.

But know, Arjuna, my other, higher nature — the life-principle by which this whole universe is held up.

Understand that all beings arise from these two. I am the origin of the whole universe — and also its dissolution.

Nothing at all is higher than me, Arjuna. All this is strung on me like beads on a thread.

I am the strength of the strong — free from desire and passion. In beings I am desire that doesn't violate dharma.

Confused by these three qualities, the world doesn't recognize me — the one who is beyond them, imperishable.

Evildoers, the confused, the lowest of men, don't take refuge in me. Their knowledge is carried off by illusion; they cling to a demonic nature.

segments.text:
Four kinds of good people worship me, Arjuna: the distressed, the seeker of knowledge, the seeker of success, and the wise.

Of these, the wise one — ever steady, devoted to the One — is the best. To the wise, I am exceedingly dear, and they are dear to me.

All of them are noble, but I hold the wise one to be my very Self. With a steady mind, they are established in me alone, the highest goal.

At the end of many lifetimes, the wise person takes refuge in me, seeing "Krishna (Vasudeva) is all this." Such a great soul is very rare.

Those whose wisdom is carried off by this or that desire turn to other deities, following various practices, bound by their own nature.

Whatever form any devotee sincerely wants to worship — I make their faith in that form steady.

Endowed with that faith, they seek what they want from that deity — and get the results, which are actually given by me.

The Imperishable is the supreme Brahman. Its nature is called the Self. The offering that causes beings to come into existence is called action (karma).

The principle behind beings is perishable nature. The principle behind the gods is the cosmic Person. The principle behind sacrifice — here in the body — is me.

The person who, remembering me alone at the moment of death, leaves the body reaches my state of being. No doubt about it.

Whatever state of being a person remembers at the last moment when leaving the body — that's the state they reach, having dwelt on it constantly.

segments.text:
So remember me always, and fight. With your mind and intellect fixed on me, you will definitely come to me.

The person whose mind, disciplined by the practice of yoga, doesn't wander anywhere else reaches the supreme divine Person, Arjuna — by meditating on him.

Whoever meditates on the all-knowing, the ancient, the ruler, subtler than the subtle, the sustainer of all, of unimaginable form, sun-like in color, beyond darkness —

at the hour of death, with a steady mind, with devotion and the power of yoga, fully drawing the life-breath between the eyebrows — reaches the supreme divine Person.

That which the knowers of the Vedas call the Imperishable, that which the disciplined ascetics, free from passion, enter; for which they practice celibacy — I'll tell you about that goal briefly.

Closing all the gates of the body, confining the mind in the heart, drawing the life-breath into the head, established in yogic concentration,

uttering the single syllable "Om" (which is Brahman), remembering me — whoever leaves the body this way reaches the supreme goal.

The one who always remembers me, thinking of nothing else — for that ever-yoked yogi, I'm easy to reach, Arjuna.

segments.text:
Great souls who have come to me, having reached the highest perfection, are not reborn in this fleeting home of suffering.

From the world of Brahma the creator on down, all worlds bring return, Arjuna. But on reaching me, there's no rebirth.

Those who know that Brahma's day lasts a thousand ages, and his night also a thousand ages — they are the ones who truly know day and night.

At the coming of day, all manifestations come out of the unmanifest. At the coming of night, they dissolve back into that very unmanifest.

This same multitude of beings, coming into being again and again, dissolves helplessly at the coming of night, Arjuna, and comes forth again at the coming of day.

segments.text:
But beyond this unmanifest is another unmanifest — eternal being — which does not perish when all beings perish.

That unmanifest, called the Imperishable, is declared the supreme goal. Whoever reaches it does not return — that is my supreme abode.

Arjuna, the supreme Person, in whom all beings rest and by whom all this is pervaded, is reached by unwavering devotion alone.

Now I'll tell you the times at which yogis depart, Arjuna — to return or not to return.

Fire, light, day, the bright half of the month, the six months of the sun's northern course — departing then, the knowers of Brahman go to Brahman.

Smoke, night, the dark half of the month, the six months of the sun's southern course — the yogi going by this path reaches the moonlight and returns.

These two paths of the world — the bright and the dark — are considered eternal. By one, a person reaches no-return; by the other, return.

To you who don't criticize, I'll reveal the most profound secret — knowledge together with realization — and by knowing it you'll be free from evil.

This is the king of sciences, the king of secrets, the supreme purifier. It's directly realizable, in line with dharma, easy to practice, never lost.

People without faith in this teaching don't reach me, Arjuna. They return to the cycle of birth and death.

All this world is pervaded by me in my unmanifest form. All beings rest in me — but I don't rest in them.

And yet beings don't rest in me — behold my divine power! Supporting beings but not resting in them, my Self is the source of beings.

As the mighty wind, moving everywhere, always rests in space — in the same way, all beings rest in me. Know it.

All beings, Arjuna, merge into my nature at the end of a cycle. At the beginning of the next cycle, I send them forth again.

Taking hold of my own nature, I send forth again and again this whole multitude of beings — helpless under the force of nature.

segments.text:
But these actions don't bind me, Arjuna. I remain as if indifferent, unattached to those actions.

Under my watch, nature produces everything that moves and everything that doesn't. That's the reason the world keeps spinning, Arjuna.

Fools dismiss me when I take a human body, not knowing my higher nature as the great Lord of beings.

With futile hopes, futile actions, futile knowledge, without discrimination, they are filled with a deluding nature — demonic and deceiving.

But great souls, Arjuna, partaking of the divine nature, worship me with undistracted minds, knowing me as the imperishable source of beings.

Always praising me, striving, firm in their vows, bowing down to me with devotion, ever-yoked, they worship me.

Others, offering the sacrifice of wisdom, worship me as the One, as the distinct, as the many, facing everywhere.

I am the ritual, I am the sacrifice, I am the offering to ancestors, I am the herb, I am the mantra, I am the clarified butter, I am the fire, I am the act of offering.

I am the father of this world, and the mother, the sustainer, the ancestor. I am what is to be known, the purifier, the sacred "Om," and the Rig, Sama, and Yajur Vedas.

segments.text:
I am the goal, the sustainer, the master, the witness, the abode, the shelter, the friend; the origin, the dissolution, the ground, the storehouse, the imperishable seed.

I give heat; I hold back and pour out the rain. I am immortality and death, what is and what is not, Arjuna.

The knowers of the three Vedas, the drinkers of soma, their sins cleansed, worshipping me with sacrifices, pray for the way to heaven. Reaching the pure world of Indra, they enjoy the heavenly pleasures of the gods.

Having enjoyed that vast heavenly world, when their merit runs out, they re-enter the world of mortals. That's how, following the dharma of the three Vedas, desiring pleasures, they keep coming and going.

For those who, thinking of nothing else, worship me as ever-united, I provide what they lack and protect what they have.

Even those who, with faith, worship other deities — they too, Arjuna, are worshipping me, though not in the proper way.

Because I alone am the receiver and the Lord of all sacrifices. But they don't know me in truth, and so they fall.

Whatever you do, whatever you eat, whatever you offer, whatever you give, whatever discipline you practice — do it, Arjuna, as an offering to me.

This way you'll be freed from the bondage of action and its good and bad results. With your self yoked in the yoga of renunciation, you'll come to me, free.

I am the same in all beings. No one is hateful or dear to me. But those who worship me with devotion — they are in me, and I am in them.

They quickly become righteous and reach lasting peace. Know this for certain, Arjuna: my devotee is never lost.

Even those considered of low birth, Arjuna — women, vaishyas, shudras — taking refuge in me, reach the highest goal.

segments.text:
Listen again, Arjuna, to my supreme word, which I'll speak to you, who are dear to me, wanting your good.

Neither the hosts of gods nor the great sages know my origin. I am the source of the gods and the great sages, in every way.

Whoever knows me as unborn, without beginning, the great Lord of the worlds — that person, not confused among mortals, is freed from all sins.

Discernment, knowledge, freedom from confusion, patience, truth, self-control, calm, pleasure, pain, existence, non-existence, fear, fearlessness —

non-violence, equanimity, contentment, austerity, generosity, fame, infamy — these various states of beings come from me alone.

The seven great sages and the four ancient ones (Manus), sharing in my nature, were born of my mind. From them came all these creatures in the world.

Whoever truly understands this glory and power of mine is united with me in unshakable yoga. No doubt about it.

I am the source of everything. From me, everything comes. The wise, understanding this, worship me with devotion.

With their thoughts on me, their lives surrendered to me, telling each other about me, always speaking of me — they are content and joyful.

segments.text:
You are the supreme Brahman, the supreme abode, the supreme purifier — the eternal, divine Person, primal God, unborn, all-pervading.

This is what all the sages say — and so does the divine sage Narada, and Asita, Devala, and Vyasa. And you yourself say it to me.

I accept everything you've told me as true, Krishna. Neither gods nor demons truly understand your manifestation, Lord.

You alone know yourself by yourself, supreme Person — source of beings, Lord of beings, God of gods, Lord of the world.

Please tell me, in full, your divine glories, through which you pervade all these worlds.

How shall I know you, yogi, by constant meditation? In what various aspects should I meditate on you, Lord?

Tell me again, in detail, about your yoga and your glories, Krishna. I never get enough of listening to your nectar-like words.

Fine — I'll tell you the main ones of my divine glories, Arjuna. There's no end to the details of my manifestation.

I am the Self seated in the hearts of all beings, Arjuna. I am the beginning, the middle, and the end of all beings.

Of the Adityas I am Vishnu; of lights, the radiant sun. Of the Maruts (storm-gods) I am Marichi; of the stars, I am the moon.

Of the Vedas I am the Sama Veda; of the gods, I am Indra. Of the senses I am the mind; in living beings I am consciousness.

Of the Rudras I am Shankara (Shiva); of yakshas and rakshasas, I am Kubera. Of the Vasus I am Fire; of mountains, Meru.

Among household priests, know me as Brihaspati — the chief. Among generals I am Skanda; among bodies of water, the ocean.

Of the great sages I am Bhrigu; of sounds, I am the single syllable "Om." Of sacrifices I am the silent repetition of a mantra; of things unmoving, the Himalayas.

Of all trees I am the ashvattha (sacred fig); of divine sages, Narada; of celestial musicians, Chitraratha; of perfected beings, Kapila.

Among horses know me as Ucchaishravas, born from the churning for nectar; among royal elephants, Airavata; among humans, the king.

Among weapons I am the thunderbolt; among cows, the wish-fulfilling Kamadhenu. I am the god of love in those who reproduce; among serpents, I am Vasuki.

Of the nagas I am Ananta; of water-beings, Varuna. Of ancestors, Aryaman; among rulers, I am Yama (god of death).

Of the demons I am Prahlada; of counters, I am Time. Of animals, the lion; of birds, Garuda.

Of purifiers I am the wind; of warriors, Rama. Of fish I am the makara; of rivers, the Ganga.

Of creations I am the beginning, the end, and the middle, Arjuna. Of sciences I am the science of the Self; among debaters, I am the debate itself.

Of letters I am "A"; of grammatical compounds, the dual compound. I alone am imperishable Time; I am the Creator facing all directions.

I am all-devouring death, and the source of what is yet to come. Of feminine qualities: fame, fortune, speech, memory, intelligence, steadiness, forgiveness.

Among hymns I am the Brihat-saman; of meters, the Gayatri. Of months I am Margashirsha (late autumn); of seasons, flower-bearing spring.

Having said this, O king, the great Lord of yoga, Hari (Krishna), revealed to Arjuna his supreme lordly form —

with many mouths and eyes, with many wondrous sights, with many divine ornaments, with many divine weapons raised;

wearing divine garlands and robes, anointed with divine perfumes — all-wonderful, radiant, infinite, with faces everywhere.

If the splendor of a thousand suns were to blaze forth all at once in the sky, it might resemble the splendor of that Great Soul.

There Arjuna saw the whole universe, in all its divisions, gathered together in the body of that God of gods.

segments.text:
God, in your body I see all the gods and the hosts of all beings — Brahma the creator on his lotus, all the sages, and the divine serpents.

I see you with countless arms, bellies, mouths, and eyes — form without end, everywhere. I see neither your end, nor middle, nor beginning, Lord of the universe, universal form.

I see you with crown, club, and discus — a mass of brilliance blazing everywhere, hard to look at on every side, with the brilliance of blazing fire and sun, beyond measure.

You are the Imperishable, the supreme to be known; you are the final resting place of this universe. You are the eternal guardian of dharma; you, I know, are the primal Person.

Without beginning, middle, or end; of infinite power; of countless arms; with the sun and moon as your eyes, a blazing fire as your mouth, scorching this universe with your radiance — I see you.

The space between sky and earth, and all the directions, are filled by you alone. Seeing this wondrous, terrifying form of yours, the three worlds tremble, Great Soul.

segments.text:
The hosts of gods enter you. Some, afraid, praise you with folded hands. The great sages and perfected beings cry "may it be well!" and sing your glory in abundant hymns.

The Rudras, Adityas, Vasus, Sadhyas, Vishvedevas, the two Ashvins, the Maruts, the ancestors, the hosts of gandharvas, yakshas, demons, and perfected beings — they all gaze at you in wonder.

Seeing your great form, with many mouths and eyes, Arjuna, with many arms, thighs, feet, many bellies, many fearsome tusks — the worlds tremble, and so do I.

Seeing you touching the sky, blazing with many colors, mouths wide, great flaming eyes — my inmost self trembles; I find no firmness or peace, Vishnu.

Seeing your mouths, terrible with tusks, like the fires of cosmic dissolution, I lose my bearings and find no refuge. Be gracious, Lord of gods, home of the worlds.

Into you rush all the sons of Dhritarashtra, along with the hosts of kings — Bhishma, Drona, even Karna, and our chief warriors too —

hurrying into your mouths, terrifying with tusks, horrifying to behold. Some, caught between the tusks, are seen with their heads ground to powder.

segments.text:
As many torrents of rivers rush toward the ocean, so do these heroes of the world of men rush into your flaming mouths.

As moths rush headlong into a blazing fire to their destruction, so these creatures rush into your mouths to their destruction.

Swallowing all the worlds on every side with your flaming mouths, you lick them up. Your fierce rays, filling the whole universe with brilliance, burn, Vishnu.

Tell me who you are, fearsome one — I bow to you, best of gods; be gracious. I want to understand you, the primal one, because I can't comprehend your purpose.

You are the primal God, the ancient Person. You are the supreme resting place of this universe. You are the knower and the known, the highest goal. By you, this universe is pervaded, infinite-formed one.

for those whose minds have entered into me, I quickly become the rescuer from the ocean of death and rebirth, Arjuna.

Fix your mind on me alone; let your intellect rest in me. Then, without doubt, you'll live in me alone.

But if you can't steadily fix your mind on me, then try to reach me through the practice of yoga, Arjuna.

If you can't even practice, devote yourself to working for me. Just performing actions for my sake, you'll reach perfection.

If you can't do even that, taking refuge in yoga toward me, give up the fruits of all your actions — with self-control.

Knowledge is better than practice; meditation is better than knowledge; renouncing the fruits of action is better than meditation; from that renunciation, peace follows right away.

The one who hates no creature, who is friendly and compassionate to all, free from "mine" and "I," even-minded in pleasure and pain, forgiving;

always content, yoked, self-controlled, firm in resolve, with mind and intellect offered to me — that devotee is dear to me.

The one who doesn't disturb the world, and isn't disturbed by the world, free from delight, anger, fear, and agitation — is dear to me.

The one who wants nothing, is pure, skilled, detached, untroubled, giving up all undertakings — that devotee is dear to me.

The one who neither rejoices nor hates, neither grieves nor desires, renouncing both good and evil, full of devotion — is dear to me.

The same toward enemy and friend, the same in honor and dishonor, the same in cold and heat, in pleasure and pain, free from attachment;

to whom blame and praise are the same, silent, content with anything, homeless, steady in mind, full of devotion — that person is dear to me.

non-attachment, not identifying with son, wife, home, and so on; and constant evenness of mind in both desirable and undesirable events;

and unswerving devotion to me through undivided yoga; going to solitary places, distaste for crowds of people;

steadiness in self-knowledge, and insight into the goal of true knowledge — this is what is called knowledge; anything opposed to it is ignorance.

I'll describe what should be known — knowing which, one attains immortality: the beginningless supreme Brahman, which is said to be neither being nor non-being.

It has hands and feet everywhere, eyes, heads, and faces everywhere, ears on all sides — it stands enveloping everything in the world.

Appearing to have the qualities of all the senses, yet free from all senses; unattached, yet supporting all; free from the qualities, yet the experiencer of the qualities.

Inside and outside all beings; unmoving and also moving; because it is so subtle, it can't be grasped; it is far away — and yet near.

Undivided, yet it seems divided among beings. It should be known as the sustainer of beings — their devourer and their creator.

segments.text:
The Light of lights, it is said to be beyond darkness. It is knowledge, the knowable, the goal of knowledge — seated in the heart of all.

So the field, knowledge, and what is to be known have been briefly described. My devotee, understanding this, attains my state of being.

Know both nature (prakriti) and Person (purusha) to be without beginning. Know also that changes and the qualities are born of nature.

Nature is said to be the cause in the production of the body and its activities. Person is said to be the cause in the experience of pleasure and pain.

For Person, seated in nature, experiences the qualities born of nature. Attachment to the qualities is the cause of birth in good and bad wombs.

The supreme Person in this body is also called the spectator, the permitter, the sustainer, the experiencer, the great Lord, the supreme Self.

Whoever understands Person and nature with its qualities in this way — however they are living — is not born again.

segments.text:
Some see the Self in themselves by themselves through meditation; others by the yoga of analysis (Sankhya), and others by the yoga of action.

Yet others, not knowing, worship as they have heard from others. They too cross beyond death, sticking to what they have heard.

Arjuna, whatever being is born — moving or unmoving — know that it arises from the union of the field and the knower of the field.

Whoever sees the supreme Lord dwelling equally in all beings, imperishable among the perishing — truly sees.

For seeing the same Lord established everywhere, one doesn't destroy oneself by oneself — and so reaches the supreme goal.

The one who sees that all action is done by nature alone, and that the Self is therefore not the doer — truly sees.

When one sees the diverse states of beings as resting in the One, and their expansion as coming from that alone — then one attains Brahman.

Being without beginning and without qualities, the imperishable supreme Self, Arjuna, though dwelling in the body, neither acts nor is stained.

As all-pervading space, because of its subtlety, isn't stained, the Self, seated everywhere in the body, isn't stained either.

Again I'll tell you the supreme knowledge, the best of all knowledge, by knowing which the sages have gone from this world to supreme perfection.

Resorting to this knowledge, having become like me, they are not born at creation or troubled at dissolution.

My womb is the great Brahman (nature); in it I plant the seed. From that, Arjuna, all beings are born.

Whatever forms come out of any womb, Arjuna — the great Brahman is their womb, and I am the seed-giving father.

The three qualities — sattva (purity), rajas (passion), and tamas (dullness) — born of nature, tie the imperishable embodied one down to the body, Arjuna.

Among these, sattva, because of its purity, is illuminating and healthy. It binds by attachment to happiness and attachment to knowledge.

segments.text:
Know that rajas is passion, the source of thirst and attachment. It binds the embodied, Arjuna, through attachment to action.

Know that tamas, born of ignorance, confuses all embodied beings. It binds through carelessness, laziness, and sleep.

Sattva attaches a person to happiness, rajas to action, Arjuna. But tamas, veiling knowledge, attaches to heedlessness.

Sattva rises by overcoming rajas and tamas; rajas rises by overcoming sattva and tamas; and tamas rises by overcoming sattva and rajas.

When the light of knowledge streams out through all the gates of this body, know that sattva is dominant.

Greed, activity, undertaking of actions, restlessness, craving — these arise when rajas is dominant, Arjuna.

Darkness, inactivity, heedlessness, and confusion — these arise when tamas is dominant, Arjuna.

If the embodied one dies while sattva prevails, they reach the pure worlds of those who know the highest.

Dying in rajas, one is reborn among those attached to action. Dying in tamas, one is reborn in the wombs of the deluded.

The fruit of good action is said to be pure — sattvic. The fruit of rajas is pain; the fruit of tamas is ignorance.

From sattva comes knowledge; from rajas, greed; from tamas, heedlessness, confusion, and ignorance.

segments.text:
They speak of an imperishable sacred fig tree (ashvattha), with roots above and branches below. Its leaves are the Vedic hymns. Whoever knows this tree knows the Vedas.

Below and above its branches spread, fed by the three qualities of nature; its twigs are the objects of sense. Its roots, also spreading below, give rise to action in the human world.

The tree's true form isn't seen here — not its end, its beginning, or its foundation. Cutting down this firmly-rooted tree with the strong axe of non-attachment,

one should seek that place, from which, once reached, no one returns: "I take refuge in that primal Person, from whom the ancient stream of activity has flowed."

Free from pride and delusion, having conquered the evil of attachment, always abiding in the Self, desires withdrawn, freed from the pairs of opposites called pleasure and pain — the unconfused reach that imperishable abode.

Neither the sun, nor the moon, nor fire lights up that place. Having reached it, no one returns — that is my supreme abode.

An eternal part of me, becoming a living being (jiva) in the world of the living, draws to itself the mind and the five senses that rest in nature.

segments.text:
When the Lord of the body takes up a body and when he leaves it, he takes these along and moves on — as the wind carries scents from their sources.

Presiding over ear, eye, touch, taste, smell — and the mind — he experiences the objects of sense.

Whether leaving or staying, or experiencing, joined with the qualities — the confused don't see him; but those with the eye of knowledge see.

Striving yogis also see him seated in themselves. But the unintelligent, with unrefined selves, though striving, don't see him.

The radiance of the sun that lights up the whole world, and the radiance in the moon and fire — know that radiance to be mine.

Entering the earth, I sustain all beings by my power. Becoming the nourishing moon, I feed all plants.

Becoming the digestive fire in the body of breathing beings, joined with the inward and outward breaths, I digest the fourfold food.

And I am seated in the hearts of all. From me come memory, knowledge, and their loss. I alone am to be known through all the Vedas; I am the author of Vedanta and the knower of the Vedas.

There are two persons (purushas) in the world — the perishable and the imperishable. The perishable is all beings. The imperishable is called the unchanging.

But the supreme Person is another — called the supreme Self, the imperishable Lord who enters the three worlds and sustains them.

Since I transcend the perishable and am higher even than the imperishable, I am known in the world and in the Veda as Purushottama — the Supreme Person.

The one who, without confusion, knows me as Purushottama knows everything — and worships me with their whole being, Arjuna.

So, Arjuna, this most secret teaching has been given by me. Having understood it, one becomes wise, and has done all that needs to be done.

Fearlessness, purity of heart, steadfastness in the yoga of knowledge, generosity, self-control, sacrifice, study of scripture, discipline, honesty;

non-violence, truthfulness, absence of anger, renunciation, calm, not speaking ill of others, compassion for beings, freedom from greed, gentleness, modesty, steadiness;

vigor, forgiveness, strength, purity, freedom from hatred, absence of arrogance — these are the marks of a person born with divine qualities, Arjuna.

Showiness, arrogance, self-importance, anger, harshness, and ignorance — these, Arjuna, belong to a person born with demonic qualities.

Divine qualities lead to liberation; demonic qualities lead to bondage. Don't grieve, Arjuna — you were born with divine qualities.

Two kinds of beings exist in this world: the divine and the demonic. I've described the divine at length. Now listen as I describe the demonic, Arjuna.

Demonic people don't know what they should do or what they should not do. Purity, good conduct, truth — none of these is in them.

"The world has no truth," they say, "no moral basis, no God. It is produced by the union of male and female, driven by lust — what else?"

Holding such views, these lost souls — of small understanding and cruel deeds — arise as enemies of the world, aiming at its destruction.

segments.text:
Clinging to insatiable lust, full of hypocrisy, pride, and arrogance, holding corrupt ideas out of confusion, they act with impure resolve.

Consumed by endless worries ending only at death, making the satisfaction of desires their supreme goal, convinced that this is all there is —

bound by hundreds of chains of expectation, given over to lust and anger, they strive to pile up wealth by unjust means for the gratification of their desires.

"This I've got today; this desire I'll fulfill. This is mine; and this wealth will also be mine."

"That enemy I've killed; and others too I'll kill. I'm the lord; I'm the enjoyer; I'm successful, powerful, and happy."

"I'm rich and well-born. Who is equal to me? I'll sacrifice, I'll give, I'll enjoy." So, deluded by ignorance,

scattered by many anxieties, caught in the net of delusion, clinging to the gratification of desires — they fall into a foul hell.

Self-important, stubborn, drunk with wealth and pride, they perform sacrifices in name only, for show, not according to the proper way.

Given to egotism, force, insolence, lust, and anger, these spiteful people hate me — seated in their own bodies and in others.

segments.text:
These hateful, cruel evildoers, the lowest of men, I repeatedly hurl into demonic wombs in the cycle of rebirths.

Entering demonic wombs, the confused, not attaining me from birth to birth, fall from there to an even lower state, Arjuna.

Threefold is the gateway to hell, destructive of the self: lust, anger, and greed. So a person should let go of these three.

The person freed from these three gates of darkness, Arjuna, works for their own good — and by that reaches the supreme goal.

Whoever casts aside the guidance of scripture and acts under the impulse of desire reaches neither perfection, nor happiness, nor the supreme goal.

segments.text:
The faith of embodied beings is of three kinds, born of their own nature — sattvic, rajasic, and tamasic. Listen.

Each person's faith matches their nature, Arjuna. A person is shaped by their faith: as their faith is, so are they.

Sattvic people worship the gods; rajasic, the yakshas and demons; tamasic worship ghosts and spirits.

Those who practice severe austerities not prescribed by scripture, bound by hypocrisy and ego, driven by the force of desire and passion,

senseless, torturing the group of elements in the body — and me, dwelling within the body — know them to have demonic resolve.

The food that each person enjoys is also of three kinds — and so are sacrifice, austerity, and gifts. Hear the distinction.

Foods that promote long life, vitality, strength, health, happiness, and cheer — savory, substantial, satisfying — are dear to sattvic people.

Foods that are bitter, sour, salty, very hot, pungent, dry, and burning — causing pain, misery, and sickness — are dear to rajasic people.

Food that is stale, flavorless, rotten, decayed, leftover, and impure is dear to tamasic people.

segments.text:
The sacrifice offered according to proper rules by those not seeking its fruits, convinced "this must be done" — that is sattvic.

But the sacrifice offered aiming for results, or for show, Arjuna — know that to be rajasic.

A sacrifice done contrary to the rules — no food distributed, no sacred verses, no gifts, and no faith — is called tamasic.

Worshipping the gods, the twice-born, teachers, and the wise; purity, uprightness, celibacy, non-violence — these are called the austerity of the body.

Speech that causes no pain, truthful, pleasant and beneficial, and regular recitation of the scriptures — these are the austerity of speech.

Serenity of mind, gentleness, silence, self-control, and purity of disposition — this is called the austerity of the mind.

This threefold austerity, practiced with supreme faith by people who are yoked and aren't seeking results — is called sattvic.

Austerity performed with showiness for the sake of respect, honor, and reverence is called rajasic — unstable and fleeting.

Austerity practiced out of foolish notions, with self-torture, or aimed at ruining someone else — is called tamasic.

A gift given with the thought "it ought to be given," to someone who can't repay, at the proper place, time, and to a worthy person — that gift is considered sattvic.

A gift given grudgingly, or with the expectation of return, or aiming at the result — is considered rajasic.

A gift given in the wrong place, at the wrong time, to the unworthy, without respect, with contempt — is called tamasic.

"Om Tat Sat" — this is said to be the threefold designation of Brahman. By these, long ago, the brahmins, the Vedas, and the sacrifices were established.

So, beginning with "Om," acts of sacrifice, gift, and austerity as prescribed in scripture are always begun by those who know Brahman.

With the word "Tat," without aiming at results, the various acts of sacrifice, austerity, and gift are performed by those seeking liberation.

The word "Sat" is used in the sense of reality and in the sense of goodness; and for an auspicious act too, Arjuna, the word "Sat" is used.

Steadiness in sacrifice, austerity, and gift is also called "Sat"; and action for the sake of That is indeed called "Sat."

Whatever is sacrificed, given, or performed — and whatever austerity is done — without faith is called "asat," Arjuna. It is nothing here and nothing in the hereafter.

segments.text:
The sages understand renunciation as giving up desire-driven actions; the wise call tyaga the letting go of the fruits of all actions.

Some thinkers say action should be abandoned because it's flawed; others say acts of sacrifice, gift, and austerity should not be given up.

Hear my conclusion on this matter of tyaga, Arjuna. Tyaga has been declared to be of three kinds.

Acts of sacrifice, gift, and austerity should not be abandoned — they should be done. Sacrifice, gift, and austerity purify the wise.

segments.text:
But even these actions should be done while letting go of attachment and fruits — Arjuna, this is my final, decisive opinion.

Giving up an obligatory action is not proper. Giving it up out of confusion is called tamasic renunciation.

Whoever abandons action because it's painful — afraid of bodily trouble — performs rajasic renunciation; they won't get its real fruit.

But when an obligatory action is done, Arjuna, while letting go of attachment and fruit, because it simply ought to be done — that letting go is considered sattvic.

The sattvic renunciate, of steady intellect and cut-through doubts, neither hates unpleasant action nor is attached to pleasant action.

An embodied being can't give up actions completely. But whoever lets go of the fruits of action — that person is called the renouncer.

Undesired, desired, and mixed — threefold is the fruit of action that comes after death to those who haven't let go. But for those who have renounced, there's no such fruit.

segments.text:
Listen to these five causes for the accomplishment of all actions, Arjuna, as declared in the conclusions of Sankhya:

the body, the agent, the various instruments, the various and distinct activities, and divine providence as the fifth.

Whatever action a person undertakes with body, speech, or mind — right or wrong — these five are its causes.

Given that, whoever — with an untrained understanding — sees only the pure Self as the agent is dull-witted and does not see.

Whose state is free from ego, whose intellect is unstained — though they kill these people, they don't really kill, and aren't bound.

The knower, the object of knowledge, and the knowledge itself — these three move action. The instrument, the action, and the agent — these three make up action.

In Sankhya teaching, knowledge, action, and agent are each said to be of three kinds, according to the three qualities of nature. Hear about these too.

The knowledge by which one sees in all beings the one, imperishable Being — undivided in the divided — know that knowledge to be sattvic.

The knowledge that sees in all beings various distinct entities of different kinds — know that to be rajasic.

But the knowledge which clings to one effect as though it were everything, without reason, without basis in truth, and trivial — is called tamasic.

An obligatory action done without attachment, without desire or hatred, by one who seeks no fruit — is called sattvic.

But the action done with great effort by one who seeks results, or impelled by ego — is called rajasic.

An action begun out of confusion, without regard for consequences, loss, injury, or ability — is called tamasic.

Free from attachment, not talking about "me," endowed with firmness and energy, unmoved by success or failure — that agent is called sattvic.

Passionate, desiring the fruit of action, greedy, naturally harmful, impure, full of joy and sorrow — that agent is called rajasic.

Unyoked, vulgar, stubborn, deceitful, malicious, lazy, depressed, and procrastinating — that agent is called tamasic.

Now listen, Arjuna, to the threefold division of intellect and steadiness according to the qualities — I'll tell you fully and separately.

The intellect that knows action and restraint from action, what should be done and what shouldn't, fear and fearlessness, bondage and liberation, Arjuna — that is sattvic.

The intellect that doesn't rightly understand what is dharma and what is not, what should be done and what shouldn't — that, Arjuna, is rajasic.

The intellect that, enveloped in darkness, takes unrighteousness for righteousness and sees everything the wrong way — that is tamasic, Arjuna.

The unwavering steadiness with which, through yoga, a person controls the functions of the mind, life-breaths, and senses — that steadiness is sattvic.

The steadiness with which a foolish person doesn't give up sleep, fear, grief, depression, and conceit — that steadiness, Arjuna, is tamasic.

Now hear from me, Arjuna, the three kinds of happiness that come after long practice and bring the end of pain.

The happiness that seems like poison at first but turns into nectar in the end, born of the clear insight of the Self — is called sattvic.

The happiness that arises from sense contact — like nectar at first, poison in the end — is considered rajasic.

Happiness that confuses the self from beginning to end, coming from sleep, laziness, and heedlessness — is called tamasic.

There is no being on earth or even in heaven among the gods who is free from these three qualities born of nature.

The duties of brahmins, warriors, merchants, and laborers, Arjuna, are distributed according to the qualities born of their own nature.

Calm, self-control, austerity, purity, patience, uprightness, knowledge, realization, and faith in the spiritual — these are the natural duties of the brahmin.

But the steadiness by which one holds on to dharma, pleasure, and wealth, Arjuna, with attachment and craving for fruits — that steadiness is rajasic.

The steadiness with which a foolish person doesn't give up sleep, fear, grief, depression, and conceit — that steadiness, Arjuna, is tamasic.

Now hear from me, Arjuna, the three kinds of happiness that come after long practice and bring the end of pain.

The happiness that seems like poison at first but turns into nectar in the end, born of the clear insight of the Self — is called sattvic.

The happiness that arises from sense contact — like nectar at first, poison in the end — is considered rajasic.

Happiness that confuses the self from beginning to end, coming from sleep, laziness, and heedlessness — is called tamasic.

There is no being on earth or even in heaven among the gods who is free from these three qualities born of nature.

The duties of brahmins, warriors, merchants, and laborers, Arjuna, are distributed according to the qualities born of their own nature.

Calm, self-control, austerity, purity, patience, uprightness, knowledge, realization, and faith in the spiritual — these are the natural duties of the brahmin.

segments.speaker: Krishna
segments.instructions: Read this clearly and naturally, at a normal conversational pace. Calm, confident, warm. Don't emphasize words dramatically; just deliver the line plainly like a teacher speaking to a student.
segments.text:
Valor, vigor, firmness, skill, not running from battle, generosity, and a leader's nature — these are the natural duties of the warrior.

Farming, cattle-rearing, and trade are the natural duties of the merchant. Work consisting of service is the natural duty of the laborer.

A person reaches perfection by being engaged in their own duty. Hear how one engaged in their own duty reaches perfection.

By worshipping, through his own work, the One from whom the activity of all beings flows and by whom all this is pervaded — a person reaches perfection.

Better to do your own duty imperfectly than someone else's duty perfectly. Doing the work that comes from your own nature, you don't incur sin.

Don't abandon the duty you are born to, Arjuna, even if it is flawed. Every undertaking is wrapped in some flaw, as fire is wrapped in smoke.

With an intellect unattached everywhere, with the self under control, free from desire — through renunciation one attains the supreme perfection of freedom from action.

How, once perfected, one reaches Brahman — the supreme culmination of knowledge — hear from me in brief, Arjuna.

segments.text:
With a pure intellect, restraining the self by firmness, giving up sound and the other sense-objects, setting aside attraction and aversion;

living in solitude, eating lightly, controlling body, speech, and mind, always intent on the yoga of meditation, taking refuge in dispassion;

giving up ego, force, arrogance, desire, anger, and possession; free from "mine," at peace — one becomes fit to become Brahman.

Having become Brahman, serene in self, one neither grieves nor desires. The same toward all beings, one attains supreme devotion to me.

Through devotion one comes to know me — how great I am, who I truly am. Knowing me in truth, one enters into me at once.

Performing all actions, always taking refuge in me, by my grace one reaches the eternal, imperishable abode.

Mentally surrendering all actions to me, with me as the highest goal, resorting to the yoga of the intellect — always fix your mind on me.

With your mind on me, you'll cross all difficulties by my grace. But if, out of ego, you won't listen, you'll be lost.

If, clinging to ego, you think "I will not fight" — your resolve is useless. Your nature will compel you.

segments.text:
Bound, Arjuna, by your own karma born of your nature, you will helplessly do what, out of confusion, you don't want to do.

The Lord lives in the hearts of all beings, Arjuna, making all beings spin as if mounted on a machine, by his illusory power.

Take refuge in him alone, with all your being, Arjuna. By his grace you'll attain supreme peace and the eternal abode.

So the wisdom more secret than any secret has been told to you by me. Reflect on it fully — then do as you wish.

Listen again to my supreme word — the most secret of all. You are deeply loved by me; so I'll tell you, for your welfare.

Fix your mind on me, be devoted to me, offer to me, bow to me. You'll come to me — truly, I promise — because you are dear to me.

segments.speaker: Krishna
segments.instructions: Read this warmly at natural pace. Plain delivery.
segments.text:
Let go of all dharmas and take refuge in me alone. I will free you from all sins. Do not grieve.

Never share this teaching with someone without discipline, without devotion, who doesn't want to listen, or who speaks ill of me.

Whoever teaches this supreme secret to my devotees, practicing supreme devotion to me, will without doubt come to me.

Among humans, no one does more precious service to me than that person, and no one on earth will be more beloved by me.

And whoever studies this righteous dialogue between us — by them, I shall be worshipped through the sacrifice of knowledge. That's my conviction.

Even the person who, full of faith and without ill-will, simply listens to it — even they, liberated, will attain the auspicious worlds of the meritorious.

Have you heard this, Arjuna, with a one-pointed mind? Has the delusion born of your ignorance been destroyed?

segments.speaker: Sanjaya
segments.instructions: Read this in an even, straightforward narrator's voice, at a normal conversational pace. Plainly stated, no dramatic emphasis.
segments.text:
Thus I heard this dialogue between Krishna and the noble Arjuna — wonderful, making the hair stand on end.

By the grace of Vyasa, I heard this supreme secret of yoga directly from Krishna, the Lord of yoga, who declared it in person.

O king, as I recall again and again this wondrous and sacred dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna, I rejoice again and again.

And as I remember again and again that most wondrous form of Hari (Krishna), my wonder is great, O king, and I rejoice again and again.

Wherever Krishna is — the Lord of yoga — wherever Arjuna is — the wielder of the bow — there, I am convinced, are fortune, victory, prosperity, and sound judgment.
